exemplar.title: To what extent does social conformity contribute to individuals adopting abnormal behaviors?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does social conformity contribute to individuals adopting abnormal behaviors?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and focused research question effectively embedded throughout the essay
Key concepts defined up front and carried through to maintain clarity of purpose
weaknesses: Methodology description lacks transparent selection criteria and search strategy
Minor lapses in phrasing and some informal language reduce precision
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall the introduction and question formulation are strong and focused (two strands at band 5), but the methodology is only âmostly completeâ (band 3). Balancing these gives a final mark of 4/6, reflecting clear purpose and question but uneven methodological detail.

strengths: Accurate summaries of classic studies demonstrating solid understanding
Consistent and mostly precise use of subject-specific terminology throughout
weaknesses: Over-reliance on secondary and popular sources rather than primary academic ones
Occasional simplifications in theory application (e.g. equating all obedience with âabnormalâ)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Demonstrates good knowledge and usually applies sources well (band 3-4), and uses terminology very consistently (band 5). Averaging these strands yields 4/6, marking strong understanding with room to deepen source selection.

strengths: Balanced inclusion of supporting and opposing evidence (e.g., Gamson et al.)
Logical organisation and clear evaluations at the end of each section
weaknesses: Limited synthesis across studies and superficial grouping of ethical/methodological concerns
Conclusion restates rather than integrates or weighs findings critically
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands sit firmly in the middle band (4â6), with good relevance and analysis but only moderate critical depth. A holistic 4/12 reflects a competent but not yet fully integrated critical discussion.

strengths: Formal structure in place (title page, contents, headings, references)
Consistent citation style and orderly formatting
weaknesses: Incomplete table of contents (missing page numbers)
Truncated text in places and occasional layout inconsistencies
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure and formatting largely meet conventions (band 3), but missing elements and truncated content limit clarity. A 2/4 acknowledges sound organization with notable presentation gaps.

strandSelections.reasoning: Throughout the essay the student employs discipline-specific termsâauthority, agency theory, ecological validity, demand characteristics, induced-compliance paradigmâ**accurately and with consistency**.  Occasional imprecise wording (e.g., â15yâ for volts, âbenefit of surpriseâ) stops it being flawless, so a 5 in the top band best matches the performance.

The essay states the word count, gives the title, and the extracted text shows numbered segments suggesting pagination. Citations follow a consistent style and fonts/spaces are orderly. Minor issues (split URLs, unconventional page indicators like "{3}") keep it from perfection, so a 3 in the top band is appropriate.

The essay summarises classic studies (Milgram, Zimbardo, Asch, Festinger) accurately and adds brief evaluations of limitations. **Core psychological theories are generally well understood and applied to the argument**, though some simplifications (e.g., equating all obedience with âabnormalâ behaviour) show gaps.  Source use is mostly relevant but not always critical, so high competence rather than excellence (4/6) is appropriate.

The student adopts a literature-review approach and cites around twenty sources, but **many references are popular summaries (Verywell Mind, Simply Psychology, Cleveland Clinic) rather than primary peer-reviewed articles**, and no clear selection criteria or search strategy is described.  Methods for evaluating evidence (e.g., why certain counter-studies were chosen) are implied rather than explained.  This represents a "mostly complete" but not consistently appropriate methodology, fitting the middle band at 3.

The introduction states a clear focus on how social conformity contributes to abnormal behaviour, explains why the issue matters for mental-health professionals, and links it to the writerâs personal motivation. **Key concepts (authority, in-group thinking, cognitive dissonance) are defined up front and carried through the essay**, so the reader can easily see the purpose and direction. Minor lapses in phrasing and some informal phrasing mean the communication is strong rather than flawless, so the lower mark in the top band (5) is most appropriate.

The studies chosen are clearly relevant to the research question, and the essay balances supporting and opposing evidence, showing **good (though not exhaustive) coverage**.  The reliance on secondary websites lowers the overall research quality slightly, so a solid mid-band score (5/6) is justified.

The research question â âTo what extent does social conformity contribute to individuals adopting abnormal behaviours?â â is **explicit, focused and embedded in the discussion throughout**. Each main section tackles a sub-mechanism relevant to the question, showing effective connection.  Because the wording is precise and narrow enough for an EE while still allowing critical debate, it meets the descriptors for the 5-6 band; some slight generality in the phrase âabnormal behavioursâ prevents awarding the full 6.

A formal structure (title page, table of contents, section headings, references) is present and largely conforms to EE conventions. **Some headings lack subsections and the table of contents is incomplete**, so while appropriate, it is not exemplary; 3/4 fits.

The argument is logically organised (authority â in-group â dissonance) and each section ends with a brief evaluation, yet **the conclusion largely restates findings rather than weighing their relative strength or integrating theoretical perspectives**.  The critical stance is present but moderate, aligning with the middle of the 4-6 band.

Analysis goes beyond description by discussing limitations (ethical issues, sampling, ecological validity) and linking findings back to conformity; however, **synthesis across studies is limited and some assertions (e.g., direct link from obedience to âabnormalâ) are not fully supported**.  This reflects clear but not consistently detailed analysis, meriting 4 in the 4-6 band.

annotations.comment: The student correctly provides a word count at the start, meeting formatting requirements and demonstrating attention to guidelines.
The title is clearly stated immediately, providing precise focus on the investigation topic and satisfying formatting conventions.
The research question is explicitly stated, focused, and embedded within the introduction, guiding the subsequent analysis effectively.
The inclusion of a table of contents demonstrates good organizational structure, assisting reader navigation through the report.
The table of contents lacks page numbers for each section, reducing its utility; add corresponding page references to improve presentation.
The introduction effectively links personal motivation to the broader significance of mental health, demonstrating authenticity and engagement.
Definitions rely heavily on secondary websites rather than primary academic sources; incorporating peer-reviewed definitions would strengthen knowledge application.
Ethical and methodological concerns are grouped together; separate these evaluations to clarify how each limitation specifically impacts validity.
The opening generalization in this paragraph is vague and unsupported by direct examples; ground statements in specific data for stronger analysis.
Section headings are well aligned with sub-mechanisms (authority, in-group thinking, cognitive dissonance), maintaining clear focus throughout the methodology.
Milgramâs original publication is not directly cited; include the primary Milgram (1963) source to demonstrate informed selection of methodology.
The unit â15yâ is incorrect and should be â15 Vâ; precise terminology and units are essential for methodological clarity.
The description of the Fake Shock Generator is cut off mid-sentence, disrupting coherence; ensure all procedural details are fully presented.
Quantitative data from Milgramâs findings are presented without in-text citation consistency; adopt a formal citation style for all numerical results.
The cultural bias limitation is mentioned but not elaborated; consider evaluating specific cross-cultural replications to deepen critical analysis.
The account of authority tactics in the Stanford Prison Experiment is detailed and links effectively to theoretical concepts.
The Gamson et al. study is introduced clearly, but exact participant numbers are omitted; specify sample size to enhance methodological transparency.
The paragraph ends abruptly with a continuation tag, indicating incomplete text; review and ensure all content is present for coherence.
Inclusion of Gamson et al.âs contrarian findings enhances critical depth by presenting balanced evidence against earlier experiments.
Ethical limitations are described but lack explanation of how they might bias results; analyze specific impacts rather than listing superficially.
The Asch conformity study is accurately summarized with clear explanation of design and purpose.
The results are well described, but discussion lacks mention of ecological validity; consider citing field replications to support generalizability.
Limitations are noted but remain general; propose specific emotional variables and design modifications to explore their role in dissonance.
The Vaidis et al. replication is well introduced, showcasing engagement with current research paradigms.
The conclusion reiterates study findings but lacks synthesis across different lenses; weigh the relative strengths of each study to strengthen evaluation.
The reflection on research process is candid about bias and time management challenges, showing analytical self-evaluation.
Recommendations for future time management and source access are broadly stated; specifying tools like Gantt charts or database subscriptions would sharpen the plan.
The report concludes with real-world application for mental-health professionals, underlining the studyâs relevance and practical significance.
Introducing Franzen and Maderâs replication demonstrates contemporary expansion of conformity research, enriching the argumentâs relevance.
Interpretation of Franzen and Maderâs outcomes is insightful but lacks specific statistics; include effect sizes or percentages to substantiate claims.
The cognitive dissonance section is clearly signposted with a new heading, aiding logical flow and focus.
The procedural detail of Festinger and Carlsmithâs experiment is thorough, providing readers with solid grounding in methodology.
A continuation tag indicates truncated text in this paragraph, disrupting readability; conduct a careful proofread to restore missing content.
Interpretation of cognitive dissonance findings is clear, linking theory to behavioral outcomes effectively.
The references list is comprehensive in scope but relies heavily on secondary and popular websites rather than primary journal articles.
Citation entries show inconsistent formatting (e.g., ResearchGate URL split, missing italics); standardize all references to one recognized style.
The URL rendering in the Lexicon entry is garbled by a math tag, indicating an OCR or formatting error; confirm and correct the link.
The reference list on this page remains extensive, demonstrating diligent research breadth across multiple foundational and recent sources.
An orphaned URL appears outside the main list structure; ensure all entries are part of the reference list and formatted consistently.
